coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric visualization of folic acid with a smartphone-assisted fluorescence paper device based on gadolinium and nitrogen co-doped CDs
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lingfei
Qian, Minjie
Yang, Zhen-quan
Xiao, Lixia
Gong, Xiaojuan
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facial ratiometric fluorescence nanorpobe (RFP) based on gadolinium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Gd,N-CDs) was for the first time proposed for visual detection of folic acid (FA). The Gd,N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal heating of Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 4-aminobenzoic acid (PABA). The as-prepared Gd,N-CDs had an average particle size of 10.27 ± 0.35 nm and exhibited two emission bands at 396 and 548 nm, respectively upon excitation at 340 nm. In the presence of FA, the 560 nm emission of Gd,N-CDs was reduced while the fluorescence signal at 457 nm was enhanced, and the quantitatively determination of FA was based on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F457/F560). The as-constructed RFP achieved a linear range of 0.0–30.0 μM and a low detection limit (LOD) of 3.43 nM. In particular, a distinguishable change in fluorescence colour from bright orange to blue enabled the development of a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device for FA detection with LODs of 3.06 μM and 0.83 μM, respectively. The proposed sensing platform was successfully utilised for onsite detection of FA in vegetable, fruit and yolk samples with satisfactory results were achieved. This work highlights the application potential of smartphone-integrated CDs-based RFP as a promising portable method for on-site analysis of FA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Folic acid
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Smartphone
Paper-based assay

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of FA contents in real food samples by the proposed fluorescence methods
Determination of FA contents in real food samples by HPLC analysis
Fluorescence response of Gd,N-CDs to FA
Design principle of the Gd,N-CDs-based RFP
Fluorescent papers strip for FA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image (inset in the top right: particle size distribution histogram; inset in the bottom right: a magnified HRTEM image of an Gd,N-CDs particle), (B) the FTIR spectrum and (C) the UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Gd,N-CDs. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of Gd,N-CDs under different λex (340–400 nm). The Gd,N-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was prepared in ultrapure water.

(A) The effects of concentration of Gd,N-CDs, pH and reaction time on FA detection. (B) The influence of FA concentration (0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, 11.0, 13.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, 35.0, 40.0, 45.0, 50.0, 55.0, 60.0, 65.0, 70.0, 75.0, 80.0, 85.0, 90.0 and 100.0 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of Gd,N-CDs. (C) The linear relationship between I/I0 and FA concentration. The Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) was prepared in PBS buffer (10.0 mM, pH 12.0). (D) The selectivity of Gd,N-CDs towards FA detection.

(A) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of FA, and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Gd,N-CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of Gd,N-CDs in the absence and in the presence of 20.0 μM FA under λex = 390 nm and λem = 457 nm. (C) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Gd,N-CDs with different concentrations of FA (10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0 and 50.0 μM). (D) The MS spectra of (a) Gd,N-CDs and (b) the mixture of Gd,N-CDs and FA.

(A) Visualization detection of FA (0.0–200.0 μM) by using the smartphone-assisted sensor. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Plot of B/R of smartphone-assisted sensor versus concentration of FA.

(A) Visualization detection of FA (0.0–200.0 μM) by using the colorimetric fluorescent paper strips. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Plot of B/R of test paper versus concentration of FA.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of Gd,N-CDs and the principle for FA detection.

The results of FA detection in real food samples by using the smartphone-assisted sensor. As shown in Table 2, the recovery rates calculated by RGB value were 96.29–105.70%, indicating that the colour information of the test paper recognised by a smartphone application was feasible and reliable for FA quantitation in real food samples. Furthermore, the food sample extracts were analysed by HPLC to further check the accuracy of the proposed Gd,N-CDs-based fluorescence methods. The chromatograms were shown in Fig. S8. As shown in Table S5, the results from HPLC analyses were consistent with that obtained by the above three sensing platforms, which unambiguously demonstrated that the high accuracy feature of the proposed Gd,N-CDs-based fluorescence methods.
The results of FA detection in real food samples by using the fluorescent test paper.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric visualization of folic acid with a smartphone-assisted fluorescence paper device based on gadolinium and nitrogen co-doped CDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facial ratiometric fluorescence nanorpobe (RFP) based on gadolinium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Gd,N-CDs) was for the first time proposed for visual detection of folic acid (FA). The Gd,N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal heating of Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 4-aminobenzoic acid (PABA). The as-prepared Gd,N-CDs had an average particle size of 10.27 ± 0.35 nm and exhibited two emission bands at 396 and 548 nm, respectively upon excitation at 340 nm. In the presence of FA, the 560 nm emission of Gd,N-CDs was reduced while the fluorescence signal at 457 nm was enhanced, and the quantitatively determination of FA was based on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F457/F560). The as-constructed RFP achieved a linear range of 0.0–30.0 μM and a low detection limit (LOD) of 3.43 nM. In particular, a distinguishable change in fluorescence colour from bright orange to blue enabled the development of a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device for FA detection with LODs of 3.06 μM and 0.83 μM, respectively. The proposed sensing platform was successfully utilised for onsite detection of FA in vegetable, fruit and yolk samples with satisfactory results were achieved. This work highlights the application potential of smartphone-integrated CDs-based RFP as a promising portable method for on-site analysis of FA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA), also known as vitamin B9, is an essential vitamin that widely exists in various foods such as meat, fresh fruits and vegetables [1]. As an essential nutrition component for human body, FA participates in the metabolism of genetic material and protein and helps improve human immunity [2–4]. The deficiency of FA would affect human normal physiological activities, causing neural tube defects, anemia, depression, tumors, etc [5–8]. The governments and organizations in different countries have regulated daily intake amount (DIA) of FA. For example, the DIAs of FA recommended by World Health Organization (WHO) are 200.0 μg for adults and 400.0 μg for pregnant women, respectively [9]. The DIA regulated by US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) are 240.0, 190.0, 400.0, and 200.0–400.0 μg for men, women, pregnant women and infants, respectively [10]. The DIA of FA suggested by China National Health Commission (NHC) is 400.0 μg [11]. However, it is important to note that excessive intake of FA would cause anorexia, nausea, zinc deficiency and a range of gastrointestinal syndromes [12], which means that the merits and shortages of FA are concentration-dependent. As such, the development of an efficient FA detection method is urgently needed for safeguarding human health.

Traditional detection methods established for FA detection include capillary electrophoresis [13], microbiological analysis [14], surface-enhanced raman scatting (SERS) [15], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16] and enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [17]. However, the application of these technologies are usually limited by expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation procedure, long testing period and the need for professional operation. Recently, the nanoprobes-based fluorescence methods have been validated to be capable of breaking through bottleneck and providing research ideas for the development of new analytical routes. So far, a variety of nanoprobes have been used for FA detection, including metal nanocluster [18], organic matter probes [19,20], semiconductor quantum dot [21] and carbon nanodots (CDs) [22]. Of note, the CDs-based nanoprobes stand out due to their remarkable feature in low cost, sensitivity, reliability and visualization.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of fluorescence carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm [23]. Due to their appealing characteristics in chemical stability, tuneable emission and low cytotoxicity, the CDs have found their significant place in sensing applications [24]. In aspect of using CDs nanorpobe for FA detection, only few researches have been reported. For example, Liu et al. [22] fabricated a nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs)-based nanoprobe by hydrothermal heating of salicylic acid and urea, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 28.0 nM. Zhan et al. [25]. reported a fluorescence nanoprobe based on glycidol-modified polyethylenimine (PEI–OH)–capped CDs for FA detection and obtained a LOD of 70.0 nM. Chen et al. [26]. developed a CDs nanoprobe by simply heating lactose and NaOH solution, achieving a LOD of 12.0 nM. The detection by all these methods relied on observing the fluorescence quenching degree of a single emission peak of CDs. However, the single fluorescent signal probes are easily susceptible to background interference and external interfering factors, limiting their sensitivity [27]. In this respect, the ratiometric fluorescent probes (RFPs) which provide two fluorescence signals with built-in correction ability appeared to be more charismatic [28–30]. The CDs-based RFPs equipping the advantages of both CDs and RFPs were revealed to be highly sensitive in sensing food additives such as chlortetracycline [31], pyrethroids [32], l-lysine [33], levofloxacin [34], etc. More importantly, the RFPs can be utilised for instant detection using a smartphone or a paper strip for colorimetric and fluorescence measurements, which not only shorten the analysis time and reduces the cost but also enable on-site testing [35,36]. However, only one work employing a quantum dots-CDs composite as a RFP was reported for FA detection [37] while the usage of single CDs with intrinsic dual emission as a RFP as well as the employment of a smartphone or a paper strip to assist visual sensing were not found. As such, the design of an efficient smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device is urgently needed for actual field tests.

This work constructed an efficient smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device for FA detection based on a gadolinium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Gd,N-CDs) (Scheme 1 ). The Gd,N-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 4-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) as raw materials. Among which, Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O was used as Gd donors to afford CDs with intrinsic dual emission, which is a classic, simple and popular synthetic pathway [38]. The Gd,N-CDs exhibited dual-emission at 457 nm and 560 nm when excited at 390 nm, where the signal at 457 nm significantly increased while that at 560 nm gradually decreased upon the introduction of FA. On account of this, a RFP based on Gd,N-CDs was constructed. Under the UV light, the obvious change in fluorescence colour from orange to blue could be captured by naked eyes. The significant colour change was converted to a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for FA detection. Specifically, the smartphone-integrated RFP was utilised for instant detection of FA by identifying RGB value of the fluorescent paper strips. Finally, the newly developed methods were used for FA detection in vegetable, fruit and yolk samples. As far as we aware, this is the first time on FA detection based on a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device, which would certainly open up new prospects for FA detection in food matrices.

All reagents and chemicals were in analytical purity and used without further purification. The Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O, OPD, PABA, FA, all amino acids, sucrose, glucose and d-fructose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The other chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. The ultrapure water purified by a Milli-Q plus system (Millipore S.A., Molsheim, France) was used for preparing solutions throughout the experiments. All food samples were bought from local markets at Yangzhou City, China.

The Gd,N-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method with slight modification of a previously reported method [39]. In detail, 0.052 g OPD, 0.2 g Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O and 0.12 g PABA were thoroughly mixed in 20.0 mL anhydrous ethanol with the assistance of ultra-sonication. The mixture solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50.0 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After being naturally cooled down to room temperature, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant containing Gd,N-CDs was subjected to nitrogen blowing to remove ethanol. After being re-dissolved in 10.0 mL ultrapure water, the Gd,N-CDs solution was purified by dialyzing through a dialysis membrane tube (MWCO = 500–1000 Da) soaking in 1.0 L fresh ultrapure water. The dialysis process was conducted for 3 days and the fresh ultrapure water was recharged every 24 h. The final dark-yellow solution was freeze-dried to collect dry Gd,N-CDs product.

The TEM image of Gd,N-CDs was obtained from HT 7800 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of Gd,N-CDs was recorded on a Nano Measurer 1.2. Cary 610/670 microscopic infrared spectrometer IR (Varian Ltd., Palo Alto, USA). The energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDXA) spectrum was taken with a Zeiss Sigma 300 field emission scanning electron microscope equipped with an energy scattering X-ray spectrometer (Zeiss Ltd., Oberkochen, Germen). The Gd composition analysis was conducted on an Optima 7300 DV inductively coupled plasma spectrometer (ICP-OES) (PerkinElmer Ltd., Boston, USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were collected by a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu Ltd., Kyoto, Japan) and a UV-1900i spectra photometer (Shimadzu Ltd., Kyoto, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence decay curves were acquired on a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Co., Ltd., England). The mass (MS) spectra of Gd,N-CDs before and after mixing with FA were acquired from a maXis ultrahigh resolution time-of-flight mass spectrometer (Bruker Ltd., Karlsruhe, Germen).

A Gd,N-CDs-based RFP was proposed for the detection of FA. The Gd,N-CDs solution at a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL was prepared in PBS buffer (10.0 mM, pH 12.0). The linear range of the proposed RFP was assessed by adding different amounts of FA into a 2.0 mL Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL). The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of a Gd,N-CDs solution before and after mixing with FA (0.0–100.0 μM) at emission wavelength (λem) 457 nm and 560 nm were F0-457/F0-560 and F457/F560, which were denoted as I0 and I, respectively. The excitation wavelength (λex) was set as 390 nm. A calibration curve was obtained by plotting I/I0 versus FA concentration. The LOD was obtained on the basis of 3δ/slope rule.

To observe the selectivity of the Gd,N-CDs-based RFP towards FA, the fluorescence response of a Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) towards 0.01 mM FA and 0.1 mM the other possible interfering species that may co-exist in real food samples were studied. The possible interferences include fourteen kinds of amino acids (dl-methionine, β-hydroxyalanine, arginine, l-threonine, histidine, glycine, leucine, glutamic acid, alanine, tryptophan, aspartic acid, phenylalanine, L-methionin, dl-cysteine), five kinds of small molecules (glutathione, glutathiol, sucrose, glucose, d-fructose), thirteen kinds of anions (C2O4 2−, Br−, I−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, Cl−, HPO4 2−, HSO3 −, NO2 −, F−), seven kinds of cations (Na+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Ni3+), two kinds of FA analogs (aminopterin and methotrexate) and seven kinds of other vitamins (thiamine/VB1, niacin/VB3, sdenine/VB4, biotin/VB7, inositol/VB8, cobalamin/VB12 and ascorbic acid/VC). The above substances were added to a series of tubes containing 2.0 mL Gd,N-CDs solutions and then the I/I0 were recorded with an excitation of 390 nm.

To assess the anti-interference ability of the proposed sensing system, a series of Gd,N-CDs solutions (0.01 mg/mL) containing 0.01 mM FA were firstly prepared. Then, the above-mentioned interfering substances were introduced to mix with final contents at 0.1 mM. The I/I0 were recorded with an excitation of 390 nm.

Different amounts of FA were added into a series of aqueous solutions of Gd,N-CDs-based RPF (0.1 mg/mL) with the final concentrations at 0.0–200.0 μM. Then, the corresponding colour photographs under a 365 nm UV lamp were taken by a smartphone. In order to reduce background interference, the UV lamp was placed in a darkroom and a fixed angle and position was selected for taking photographs. The colour information of the obtained photos were distinguished and recognised as RGB values by a smartphone application (Color Coll).

Fluorescent paper sensor was developed according to a previously reported work [37]. In detail, glass fibre membranes were soaked for 24 h in a series of aqueous solutions of the Gd,N-CDs-based RPF (0.05 mg/mL) containing different contents of FA. After being removed from the Gd,N-CDs solution and naturally air dried, the as-prepared paper strips were observed under a 365 nm UV lamp. The RGB values of the paper-based sensor were analysed according to the above procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FA contents in real food samples by the proposed fluorescence methods
ce:section.ce:para: The food samples used in this work include vegetables (celery, broad bean, lettuce), fruits (citrus, pear, kiwi) and eggs (yolk). Before extraction, the vegetables were firstly chopped, the peels of fruits were scraped and then cut into pieces, and the yolk was stirred and mixed evenly. Then, 10.0 g of each pre-treated samples were weighed and transferred into centrifuge tubes containing 20.0 mL ultrapure water. After being soaked for 20.0 min, the sample extractions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the supernatants were collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm cellulose acetate syringe filter to obtain the sample extracts.

For the sample analyses by the as-constructed RFP, 20.0 μL of each sample extract was added into a 2.0 mL Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) and thoroughly mixed by handshaking. The I/I0 was determined with a fluorescence spectrometer with λex = 390 nm. For the sample analyses by a smartphone application, 80.0 μL of each sample extract was added into a 2.0 mL Gd,N-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and mixed well. The fluorescence photos were obtained and analysed by a smartphone application (Color Coll) under a 365 nm UV lamp. For the sample analyses by the as-prepared fluorescent test paper, a serious of mixture solutions of 2.0 mL Gd,N-CDs solution (0.05 mg/mL) and 80.0 μL of each sample extract were obtained. Then, the glass fibre membrane was soaked in the mixture solution for 24 h and then naturally air dried. The as-prepared paper-based sensor was observed and analysed by a smartphone application (Color Coll) under a 365 nm UV lamp. For the above sample analyses, the FA standard solutions at three concentration levels (5.0, 10.0 and 15.0 μM) were spiked into the sample extracts to assess the quantitative recoveries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of FA contents in real food samples by HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the accuracy of the fluorescence sensing system, the above sample extractions were also analysed by HPLC. The measurement was carried out on a Waters e2695 series HPLC coupled with a 2998PDA detector. An Agilent ZORBAX SB–C18 (250 × 4.6 mm id stainless steel, 5 μm) was used for separation. The mobile phase was composed of the prepared inorganic salt solution and methanol (92: 8, V/V). The inorganic salt solution was prepared as follows: 6.8g KH2PO4 was added into 70.0 mL KOH aqueous solution (0.1 M), dilute to 920.0 mL with double distilled water and the pH was adjusted to 6.3 ± 0.1. The elution was conducted under isocratic elution model. The temperature was set at 30 °C, and the flow rate was set at 1.0 mL min−1. The detection wavelength was at 283 nm.

The successful fabrication of fluorescent Gd,N-CDs was implemented by one-step hydrothermal heating of OPD, Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O and PABA. The PL properties of the as-fabricated Gd,N-CDs depend on the molar ratios of reaction precursors, and quantum yield (QY) is a important parameter for determining the characteristics. As revealed in Fig. S1, the Gd,N-CDs synthesised with different molar ratios of OPD, Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O and PABA exhibited different PL properties. Since the Gd,N-CDs synthesised with 1:1:2 M ratio of OPD, Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O and PABA had the highest QY of 2.52%, (Table S1), it was used as fluorescence nanoprobe for subsequent sensing applications.

The morphology and size of Gd,N-CDs was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared Gd,N-CDs were uniformly dispersed on the TEM grid with spherical shape. To obtain the particle size distribution histogram, the diameters of 80 particles on various spots of TEM images were measured by Nano Measurer software (v 1.2). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1A, the particle sizes of the Gd,N-CDs species were dispersed in a range of 8.0–12.0 nm, and the average particle size was calculated to be 10.27 ± 0.35 nm. The inset of Fig. 1A depicted a magnified HRTEM image of Gd,N-CDs. A lattice spacing of ca. 0.25 nm indicated that the Gd,N-CDs had a graphitic structure, which is consistent with the (100) facet of graphitic structure [40].

The functional information of Gd,N-CDs were characterised by acquiring its FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B). The broad absorption band at 3445–2741 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H. The absorption peak at 1614 cm−1 was originated from the stretching vibration of CC on the aromatic ring. The peaks at 1379 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–O, C–N and C–H, respectively. The absorption peak at 747 cm−1 was due to the out-of-plane bending vibration of C–H. It was clearly that the Gd,N-CDs was functionalised with amino, amide and carboxylic acid groups which afforded its excellent hydrophilicity. The elemental composition was assessed by EDXA by dropping aqueous solution of Gd,N-CDs on Si grid. As shown in Fig. S2 and Table S2, the Gd,N-CDs was composed of 55.40% C, 12.56% O, 21.31% N and 10.73% Gd. The content of Gd evaluated by ICP-OES was 11.17% (Table S2), which was consistent with the results from EDXA. In addition, the Gd contents of the Gd,N-CDs derived from different molar ratios of OPD, Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O and PABA evaluated by ICP-OES showed that the introduction of higher molar ratio of Gd(NO3)3⋅6H2O resulted in higher Gd content in the as-prepared CDs (Table S1). The above information clearly indicated that Gd and N were successfully doped into CDs.

The optical properties of Gd,N-CDs were characterised by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Gd,N-CDs (blue line, Fig. 1C) revealed three typical absorption bands at 268, 274 and 385 nm, where the former two were due to π→π* transitions of CC bonds on the aromatic ring skeleton [41] and the faint peak at 385 nm was ascribed to n→π* transition of CO bonds [42]. The fluorescence emission spectrum of Gd,N-CDs (red line, Fig. 1C) revealed that the Gd,N-CDs showed an emission peak at 457 nm and 560 nm under λex = 390 nm. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of Gd,N-CDs (black line, Fig. 1C) showed that the Gd,N-CDs had two excitation peaks at 390 nm and 429 nm with 390 nm as the maximum one. Furthermore, the emission spectra of Gd,N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths were evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 1D, when excited at 340 nm, the Gd,N-CDs exhibited two emission bands at 396 and 548 nm, respectively, with the former one much higher than the later one. With the increasing of the excitation wavelength from 340 to 390 nm, the former band continuously red-shifted with the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased. On the contrary, the later peak continuously red-shifted with the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, and the maximum value was obtained at 560 nm with 390 nm excitation wavelength, which was about 8 times that of the fluorescence intensity of the former peak at 457 nm.

The sensing conditions including Gd,N-CDs concentration, pH and reaction time were optimised to obtain the optimal detection performance. The effect of Gd,N-CDs concentration on I/I0 was firstly studied. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the I/I0 obtained at a concentration range of 0.001–0.01 mg/mL was higher than that obtained when the concentration of Gd,N-CDs was above 0.01 mg/mL. To ensure sufficient fluorescence intensity for sensing application, the fluorescence intensity of Gd,N-CDs at various concentrations were obtained. As shown in Fig. S3, a concentration of Gd,N-CDs at 0.01 mg/mL could afford sufficient fluorescence intensity for subsequent experiments. As such, 0.01 mg/mL was chosen as the optimal Gd,N-CDs concentration.

The effect of pH on I/I0 was assessed by observing the changes of I/I0 with pH in a range of 2.0–12.0. As shown in Fig. 2A, the I/I0 was relatively low in the pH range of 2.0–12.0 even though the values fluctuated with the variation of pH whereas a dramatic increase of I/I0 was observed when pH reached at 12.0. The fluctuation of I/I0 with pH is due to the changes of CDs fluorescence with pH resulted from the variations of CDs surface charge states with pH, which can be verified by the zeta potential values of CDs (Fig. S4) at different pHs (2.0–12.0). Therefore, pH 12.0 was selected as the optimal pH for subsequent studies.

Then, the effect of reaction time on I/I0 was also investigated. Fig. 2A showed that the I/I0 increased rapidly within 1.0 min after the addition of 50.0 μM FA into a Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL), and further lengthening the reaction time did not cause obvious change of I/I0. This indicated that the reaction could be completed rapidly within 1.0 min, i.e., the detection can be simultaneously realized after FA was mixed with Gd,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Gd,N-CDs to FA
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimised sensing conditions, the fluorescence response of Gd,N-CDs to different concentrations of FA was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2B, when the content of FA increased from 0.0 to 100.0 μM, the fluorescence emission peak of Gd,N-CDs at 457 nm significantly increased while the emission peak at 560 nm gradually decreased. After mixing with FA, the QY of Gd,N-CDs/FA mixture (4.24%) was found to be higher than that of Gd,N-CDs only (2.52%) (Fig. S5). As described in Fig. 2C, a good linear relationship between FA concentration and I/I0 was observed in a concentration range of 0.0–30.0 μM. The fitted equation was I/I0 = 0.4561[C]+1.0559 with an excellent coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9954, where [C] referred to the concentration of FA. According to the 3δ rule, the LOD was calculated to be 3.43 nM.

The analytical performance of the proposed sensing platform was compared to the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence detection methods. As showed by Table S3, the LOD of our proposed method (3.43 nM) was 1‒2 orders of magnitude lower that achieved by any of the previously reported CDs-based fluorescence methods, indicating the ultrahigh sensitivity feature of our work. Specifically, since only one type of nanoparticle, i.e., Gd,N-CDs, was employed to construct the RFP, our proposed method exhibited additional merits of simplicity and low cost.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of a sensing system are important factors for validating the proposed detection method. To observe the selectivity of Gd,N-CDs towards FA, the fluorescence response of a Gd,N-CDs solution (0.01 mg/mL) towards 0.01 mM FA and 0.1 mM the other possible interfering species (various kinds of amino acids, small molecules, anions, cations, FA analogs and other vitamins) that may co-exist in real food samples were studied. As shown in Fig. 2D, only FA had a significant effect on I/I0 while the other substances did not cause obvious change on I/I0, indicating that the Gd,N-CDs-based sensing system had excellent selectivity towards FA.

To assess the anti-interference ability of the proposed sensing system, a series of Gd,N-CDs solutions (0.01 mg/mL) containing 0.01 mM FA were prepared. Then, 0.1 mM of the above-mentioned interfering substances were introduced to mix. As can be seen from Fig. S6, the introduction of the possible interferences did not cause significant change of I/I0 of the Gd,N-CDs/FA system, implying that the proposed method had good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the Gd,N-CDs-based RFP
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism behind the changes of the fluorescence intensities of Gd,N-CDs at 457 and 560 nm were investigated. Firstly, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA (Fig. 3 A, green curve) and the excitation (Fig. 3A, red curve) and emission spectra (Fig. 3A, black curve) of Gd,N-CDs were firstly acquired. It was observed that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FA hardly coincides with the excitation and emission spectra of Gd,N-CDs, which excluded the possible mechanism from inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [43]. Then, the fluorescence property of FA itself was investigated. As depicted in Figs. S7 and S5, the FA (0.0–200.0 μM) exhibited very low emission intensity (QY = 0.11%) at 440 nm upon excitation at 390 nm which excluded the enhanced fluorescence intensity of Gd,N-CDs at 457 nm from the inherent fluorescence property of FA. Furthermore, the fluorescence decay curves of Gd,N-CDs in the presence of different contents of FA were acquired and depicted in Fig. 3B. Table S4 showed the fitted results by a two-exponential equation (See Supplementary Material). It was observed that the average lifetimes of Gd,N-CDs before and after mixing with 20.0 μM FA were 2.68 and 4.20 ns, respectively, indicating that the interaction between Gd,N-CDs and FA increased the fluorescence lifetime of Gd,N-CDs. To explore if the change of the Gd,N-CDs fluorescence lifetime was due to the formation of new compound, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Gd,N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of FA were acquired. As shown in Fig. 3C, when FA was added to the Gd,N-CDs solution, the characteristic absorption peak of FA appeared at 257 nm, 280 nm and 365 nm, respectively. However, no shifts of the peak positions were observed with the increase of FA content, which indicates that no new substance was formed. We deduce that the changes of the fluorescence intensities of Gd,N-CDs at 457 and 560 nm were most likely due to the reason that the FA (polar functional groups such as –OH, –COOH and –NH2) combined with the Gd,N-CDs surface functionalities (‒COOH and –NH2 groups) via hydrogen bonding and induced the changes of Gd,N-CDs surface functionalities [44]. To verify our inference, the MS spectra of Gd,N-CDs before and after mixing with FA were acquired (Fig. 3D), respectively. The obvious difference in MS pattern of Gd,N-CDs and Gd,N-CDs/FA unambiguously demonstrated that the addition of FA caused the structural changes of Gd,N-CDs. Based on the above information, it is believed that the changes of fluorescence signals of Gd,N-CDs were most likely due to the structural changes of Gd,N-CDs caused by FA.

The as-constructed RFP present continuous colour from orange to blue under UV light (365 nm) with the FA concentration increased from 0.0 to 200.0 μM (Fig. 4 A), which could be used as the premise for FA assay by a smartphone application via colour recognition function. Firstly, the photos of Gd,N-CDs solutions containing different concentrations of FA were taken with a smartphone under UV irradiation. Then, the colour information of the as-constructed RFP were recognised by a smartphone application and converted to the corresponding RGB values. The blue channel and red channel were selected to calculate the RGB ratio (B/R) in order to minimise interference from external environment during photo-shooting [36]. The B/R value and FA concentration showed a good linear relationship in a concentration range of 0.0–100.0 μM (Fig. 4B) and the calculated LOD was 3.06 μM, which enabled quantitation of FA with only a portable smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent papers strip for FA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate the visualization of FA detection, the as-constructed RFP solution was prepared into a paper-based fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 5 A, when FA aqueous solution dropped on the paper sensor, the fluorescence colour of the paper sensor under a 365 nm UV light gradually changed from orange to blue with an increase in FA concentration from 0.0 to 200.0 μM. For quantitation of FA by the as-constructed test paper, the corresponding RGB values were obtained by a smartphone application (Fig. 5B). A good linearity between FA concentration and B/R value was observed in a concentration range of 0.0–100.0 μM and the corresponding LOD was 0.83 μM. These data suggest that fluorescent test paper integrated with a smartphone can realise the quantitative detection of FA.

The practicability of the proposed Gd,N-CDs-based fluorescence methods for FA detection in real samples was researched by using various vegetable, fruit and yolk samples. The data recorded by the as-constructed RFP with a fluorescence spectrometer and by a smartphone application. As shown by Tables S5 and 1
, the analyses results from the two methods were in good agreement and both the two methods achieved satisfactory spiked quantitative recoveries, which were 96.52–104.92% and 96.17–106.14%, respectively. The results approved that the reliability and feasibility of the as-constructed RFP and the developed smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform for FA detection in real food samples. Meanwhile, the application of fluorescent test paper for the detection of FA contents in the above food samples was investigated and the RGB values of the test paper colour were acquired with a smartphone application.

In summary, a simple visualization-enabled ratiometric sensing platform was fabricated for FA detection based on a dual emissive Gd,N-CDs. The introduction of FA resulted in the reduction of fluorescence signal at 560 nm and the enhancement of fluorescence signal at 457 nm. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the two peaks was related to the concentration of FA added. Based on this, a RFP based on Gd,N-CDs was proposed to detect FA. A linear range of 0.0–30.0 μM and a LOD of 3.43 nM were obtained. Simultaneously, the fluorescence colour changed significantly from bright orange to blue upon the addition of FA, laying a foundation for fabricating a smartphone-assisted sensing platform and a paper-based analytical device for the on-site detection of FA, achieving LODs of 3.06 μM and 0.83 μM, respectively. The proposed sensing platforms were successfully used for FA determination in vegetables, fruits and yolk samples. This is the first report on using a visualization-enabled ratiometric sensing platform for FA detection, which would certainly broadens the application range of CDs in the field of food analyses.

Lingfei Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Minjie Qian: Investigation. Zhen-quan Yang: Formal analysis. Lixia Xiao: Investigation. Xiaojuan Gong: Formal analysis. Qin Hu: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Resources, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671625), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202), Postdoctoral Research Funding Program of Jiangsu Province (2021K249B), Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042), Yangzhou Key R&D Project (Social Development), China (2020) (YZ2020063), LvYang Golden Phoenix Plan, Yangzhou City (2019) are gratefully acknowledged.
